title: What Makes Jenny Hoyos Shorts SO VIRAL?

transcript.text: Jenny Hoyos is one of the most prolific short form content creators on YouTube. She's only 19 years old and she's generated over three billion views, mainly on YouTube shorts, with dozens of her videos going mega viral. In this video, I analyze advice she's given to figure out what exactly she does. So, right, and how you can apply this to your own YouTube videos. Now, I'm a decently successful shorts content creator myself. Okay, I've radked in a few hundred million views across my channels. I do think I'm semiqualified to provide at least some insight here, especially because the shorts I make are faceless and the shorts she makes aren't. And I know a lot of my audience doesn't want to show their face when they make shorts. So, don't worry. No matter what niche you're in, I'm going to be giving you actionable advice on how you can improve your shorts and go viral. So, broadly speaking, this is her advice when she was asked about how she makes her videos go viral. >> It's just adding story and a twist, especially with shorts since like no one's actually having to click on your video. I think you can make a video about anything. You can make video about paint drying and you can make it entertaining if there's a story around it and if the viewer is invested. >> And to me off the bat, this is one of the most crucial parts to making a good short. Whether you're doing commentary and giving a reaction or just creating some original content, what's the story behind your video? What's the narrative? And is the narrative compelling to a lot of people? Now, Jenny goes on to talk about how she builds out these stories, and it usually has something to do with a twist. So, for example, she talks about a video she made where she cooked for strangers, but the reason why she was doing this was to raise money because her kitchen broke. Immediately, this gives the viewer something to look forward to, a payoff, if you will. But this is a payoff induced by the twist. Like, okay, sure, you can cook, but there's plenty of chefs on YouTube, but what if you had to cook in a high stakes environment? You get it, guys? But seriously, this makes the videos more entertaining. Narratives that you can add to your video can include challenges, bets, or truthfully even just talking about your story in a dramatic way. These are all things that serve to keep the viewer engaged and happy. But how do you actually hook the viewer to watch your video in the first place? >> To me, a good short is one that has a strong hook. The way I like to explain it is if it could be used as a title and thumbnail on a long form and like it will still get clicks, then it'll work for a short, you know? know. So, the hook needs to be very visual. You need to understand it without even listening to it. And it needs to be so simple, like so simply said, like you said, explain to a 5-year-old. >> A huge mistake I see people making when they just start out is making their hooks too wordy. Now, depending on how long your short is, a 3se secondond hook could already be 20% of your video. The way I like to see it is that if you only have a very limited canvas space, and you're trying to create the most beautiful painting. You're not going to doodle some BS on it, right? Well, maybe some of you freaks would. I don't know. But no, you'd probably want to make sure you're using every inch of that canvas to work towards your end goal. And that's exactly how you should view shorts. Nobody has the time or quite frankly the attention span to hear you yap for 5 seconds without getting to the point. The hook has to be concise and easy to understand. And something important here to take note of is that she did say without the audio, the visual hook alone should be enough to draw the viewer in. So, when you're making your videos, mute your videos. See if you're still interested just by watching your hook. Are your visuals eye-catching enough? Can somebody tell what's going on without even hearing you talk? Now, this is another interesting thing here I want you guys to pay attention to. Jenny said she actually puts her scripts through a readability checker. And what this tells her is the reading level of the script. So, let's say, for example, it's eighth grade. She said she doesn't make any short with a reading level over fifth grade. And this might seem odd, I guess, unless you're in fifth grade, but think about it, right? You want to make your videos appeal to the most amount of people possible. And to do that, you need to make sure everybody understands you. In fact, she analyzed all of Mr. Beast's successful shorts. Now, you guys may not know who this guy is, but he's decently popular. But what she found was even more shocking because after analyzing most of his popular videos, she found that his average reading level for his shorts was about first grade. So, everybody being able to understand your short is a huge thing and probably something that a lot of you guys are just not capitalizing on. Now, let's talk about how to actually find what strategies work best for you. Bear with me because this clip is going to be a little bit longer, but it is worth listening to. >> Yeah. So, I've also like deeply analyzed all of my videos. And I think that's where you learn the most. Everyone has different audiences. What works for Mr. Beast might not work for me, right? What I did is like I did my own little experiment. Like when I knew I wanted to get into shorts, I told myself I was going to upload every day for as long as possible. And I think I only got like a week or two in. It it didn't it didn't I didn't I didn't get that far. But the goal was essentially to upload as much as possible. Quantity is what makes quality. When I was doing that quantity, I would analyze my retention graph. And this is just like one example I'll just pull out for you. So there was one video after 5 days I got I think it was like 50,000 views in 5 days, right? Which I on average I usually get like a million. So I was not happy with the performance. And I pulled up the retention graph and I noticed on the last second it was a huge dip. It was 70%, 1 second later it was 45%. Like a 25% drop in 1 second. And the retention was 83%. So what I did was I went into YouTube Studio. I trimmed off that 1 second. YouTube removed this feature. By the way, YouTube, please bring it back. But just by removing that 1 second at the end, it went from 83% to 88%. And the video went flying. And that that's why like every second counts on a short like every single second because when you think about it let's say you have a 30 secondond short right if you lose 1 second that's already 97% that's three percentages you know if you have lose 2 seconds that's 6%. So when you think about it on short it's like you're not just losing 1 second you're losing double or even triple because that 1 second accumulates to 2 or 3% of the video. I don't think I need to elaborate too much on this because she did a pretty good job explaining it, but here's the basic idea, guys. Analyze your top performing videos. The swipethrough rate, the retention graph. These are usually very helpful. Analyze what you did that seemed to work for those videos. Yeah, but especially analyze your failures, too. If a video deviates from your regular views and performs poorly, what happened with it? Was the swipe rate bad? Because to me, that sort of indicates there's a problem with the hook. Was the retention bad? because that sort of indicates that you had a problem retaining the viewer and that might be closely correlated to the story being bad or not entertaining enough. Also, something you can try is making your videos replayable or even loopable. Even if only a small percent of people who watch your short rewatch it, this can boost the retention graph by a few valuable percentage points. And sometimes that's all it takes to go viral. Now, what about the video idea? What would you say is your proportion of like video ideas to videos you actually create? >> Oh gosh. Right now in a Google doc I have a list of a thousand ideas. >> Crazy. >> And I'm only doing 10. >> Not a thousand hooks. A thousand different video ideas that could each have multiple different hooks within them. >> Yes. >> I see a lot of people making this mistake. So sometimes people, especially if they're doing commentary shorts or something, they'll just scroll, find the first good video they see, and make a video on it. And that's fine. That's cool. Nothing wrong with that, my man. But something you could try is being a little bit more picky with what you actually pull the trigger on. You know, if you made five scripts and chose the best one all the time, you might end up with a higher quality of video on your channel. Now, obviously, you still need to upload consistently no matter what, but play around with this. If the script isn't really hitting to you or you just have a bad feeling about it, it's okay to like, don't get me wrong, but it's also okay sometimes to just make another video. Do not feel obligated to post videos that you don't feel 100% about, even if you put a lot of work into them. Now, how long should you be trying to retain the viewers for a quote unquote good short? Well, I was in uh I was in Patty Galloway's accelerator and he showed some research that he had done to show like depending on your short length, this is the bar for retention you should be shooting for. So, I'm guessing that 90% retention is also related to the average total length of your videos. Whereas, if it was shorter, maybe you need retention to be higher to achieve the same virality. >> Exactly. Yes. Correct. For my personal channel, like I said, like there's no specific number. I think everyone just has to check out their analytics, but for me, I noticed if a short is less than 30 seconds, it needs to it has to have over 100% retention or else it's not going to take off. So, that's why I like to make it slightly longer, but too long is too long for my audience since I do have a very young audience. >> So, I already did make a video on this and if I can remember, I'll try to link it down below, but basically, I went into this data compiled by Patty Galloway and talked about what it meant for your own shorts. If you're interested in that, you can watch the video after this one. Dude, pump the brakes, homie. Don't click away just yet. Now, anyways, the TLDDR for that video was this pretty much high retention, good, high swipe rate, good. But more importantly, videos that are longer can go more viral, provided that the retention is good enough. So, in simple terms, 45se secondond retention on a 50-second video has the potential to go more viral than a 25-second retention on a 20-se secondond video, even though you're keeping more people retained for more than 100% of the short length. And that's just because YouTube wants to keep people on their platform as long as possible. And that's why they reward your videos that retain viewers longer. Another great way you can retain viewers longer, like I said earlier, is just to have a good story. And Jenny says, "One of the most valuable things you can do is foreshadowing." Yes, I always have spoken time to foreshadowing it. Like it doesn't matter what video idea it is. Like let me just pull up like a random idea like I'm going to the beach, right? And then it's like, why would someone watch? So that's the hook. I'm going to the beach. And what's the foreshadow? I'm going to the beach and I'm going to surprise someone with $100 at the end of the video. It could literally be with anything. It just needs to have some sort of expectation. >> So, keep that in mind when you're making your shorts. Whether it's your own original content or content you're just transforming, the viewer would be watching to the end because there's some sort of expectation, right? Some sort of payoff. And she had some more great advice when talking about how to retain the viewer with a script. >> It's just like problem solution or but therefore storytelling. But therefore, but therefore storytelling simply put is just lots of change in the story. So a story stories can't be stories without change. For example, I went on a walk, then it started raining, then I went back home. It was much more boring than if it was like, I went on a walk, but it started raining. Therefore, I started running back home and I it was raining all over me. But good thing I had a handy umbrella while I was on my way back, therefore I got home. Like it's the exact same story, but it just sounds so much more intriguing when you have like butt and then so put simply, I guess, here's the final thing I want to say. If you want to go viral like Jenny, make your videos more entertaining. If you guys like this video, leave a like or don't, whatever. If you want to make money from your YouTube shorts without even being monetized, check out the video I made on music deals because a lot of people don't know about them. You can literally get paid just by using certain background music. And I hope this video helped at least some of you guys out. If you stay to the end, I love you.
